perturbation.original_text: The undersigned acknowledge that each shall be responsible for the timely filing of such amendments and for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning him or it contained herein or therein, but shall not be responsible for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning the others, except to the extent that he or it knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned acknowledge that each shall be responsible for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning him or it contained herein or therein.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'but shall not be responsible for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning the others, except to the extent that he or it knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate,' the joint filing statement now suggests that each party is responsible for all information, even that provided by others. This contradicts SEC rules regarding the responsibilities of joint filers.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-1(k)(1)(iii) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 specifies that no person shall be responsible for the completeness or accuracy of the information concerning the other persons making the filing, unless he knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate. The modified text removes this limitation of responsibility, potentially exposing each filer to liability for inaccuracies made by other filers, even if they had no reason to suspect the inaccuracy, contradicting this rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts SEC rules, as explained in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. By removing the clause that limits responsibility for the accuracy of information provided by other filers, the changed text implies that each party is responsible for all information, regardless of its source or their knowledge of its accuracy. This contradicts the principle that filers are not responsible for others' information unless they know or have reason to believe it is inaccurate. While the scraped snippets do not directly contain the rule mentioned in the law explanation, the Law explanation provides the relevant legal citation: Rule 13d-1(k)(1)(iii) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The removal of the limitation exposes filers to liability, thus creating a contradiction.
